title: Untitled
research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAV) that help pedestrians understand the vehicle's intended behavior at crosswalks, improve their receptivity toward FAVs, and affect their crossing behavior
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment with 30 participants who walked across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Four visual features (walking silhouette, braking text, smiling curve, no signal) and four audible features (horn, music, verbal message, no signal) were tested in a 4x4 randomized complete block design. Data collected through surveys including baseline receptivity questionnaire, demographic questionnaire, pedestrian behavior questionnaire (PBQ), personal innovativeness scale, and after-study receptivity survey, plus simulator-collected objective measures for crossing time and waiting time.

main_claims.claim_text: Pedestrians receptivity toward FAVs significantly increased with the inclusion of external features
Walking silhouette and braking text were the most favored visual interfaces while verbal message was the preferred audible feature
Females and people from 30+ age group reacted most positively to the external features
Pedestrians who commit errors or show aggressive behaviors rated FAVs poorly even with external features
Pedestrians who intentionally violate traffic rules or get distracted became more cautious with FAVs and appreciated external features

limitations.text: Study designed for non-signalized intersection with single controlled scenario where FAV came from one side only
No other traffic included in virtual environment to eliminate distractions and ensure focus on features
Proposed features indicated safe crossing conditions only - vehicle always stopped; did not consider feature termination or vehicle restart scenarios
Sample size of 30 participants limits generalizability - first exposure to FAV may not reflect long-term behavior
Only one trial condition used - does not account for varying traffic situations signals vehicle operators or complex pedestrian behaviors

notes: VR setting validated as effective for FAV-pedestrian interaction research; average walking speed in simulator was 1.54 m/sec faster than general walking speed 1.20 m/sec; visual feature effect size (g2=0.151) about twice as strong as audible feature effect size (g2=0.086); interaction between visual and audible features significantly affected ratings (g2=0.076)
